title: Why being in the dark activates childhood terror patterns
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
The moment the lights go out and your body remembers

metaDescription:
The moment the lights go out and your body remembers 

 I was six years old when I learned that darkness meant danger. Not the kind of danger that movies...
htmlContent:
The moment the lights go out and your body remembers

I was six years old when I learned that darkness meant danger. Not the kind of danger that movies warn you about. The kind that lives inside your own house. The kind that comes from the people who are supposed to protect you.

Here's the thing. When the lights go out now, as an adult, my body doesn't care that I'm safe. It doesn't care that I'm 35 years old with locked doors and a partner sleeping next to me. My nervous system remembers. And it remembers hard.

You probably know what I'm talking about. That sudden shift when a room goes dark. The way your breath gets shallow. The way your ears perk up, scanning for footsteps that shouldn't be there. The way your muscles tense, ready to run or freeze or play dead. Right?

This isn't a character flaw. It's not you being dramatic or weak or broken. It's your nervous system doing exactly what it learned to do. And the person who taught it? That was you, back when you were small and powerless and trying to survive.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades watching how animals in the wild shake off trauma after a threat passes. They don't stay stuck. They don't carry the memory in their bodies forever. But humans? We do. Because our threats weren't always physical. They were relational. They were ongoing. They were the people who were supposed to love us, turning into the thing we feared most in the dark.

Think about it. A child who experiences abuse or neglect learns that darkness means vulnerability. It means the parent might come in angry. It means being alone with your thoughts, which are already screaming at you that you're bad, you're wrong, you're the reason this is happening. It means the sounds of the house become the sounds of danger. A creak. A footstep. A door opening.

Your nervous system built a map. And that map says: darkness equals threat. Every single time.

What the science of polyvagal theory tells us about darkness and fear

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, explained something that changed how I understand my own reactions. He showed that our nervous system has three main states: social engagement (safe and connected), fight or flight (mobilized for danger), and shutdown (freeze, collapse, dissociation).

When the lights go out, what happens for many of us with complex trauma is a rapid drop from social engagement into either fight or flight or straight into shutdown. There's no middle ground. No gradual slide. It's like stepping off a cliff.

I remember nights as a kid where I'd lie perfectly still in bed, not breathing, pretending to be asleep, because I knew that if my father came in and found me awake, things would get worse. I learned to disappear in the dark. To make myself small. To become invisible.

That pattern doesn't just go away because you moved out. It's etched into your nervous system. Your body still knows how to play dead. And it will do that, automatically, the moment the lights go out and you're alone with yourself.

Bessel van der Kolk, the author of The Body Keeps the Score, showed us that trauma isn't stored in the story we tell about what happened. It's stored in the body. In the muscles. In the fascia. In the nervous system itself. So when darkness comes, your body isn't remembering a memory. It's reliving an experience. The terror is fresh. The helplessness is real. And your adult brain, the one that knows you're safe, can't talk your way out of it.

Stay with me here. This is important. The fact that you can't logic your way out of this reaction doesn't mean you're broken. It means you're human. And it means your nervous system needs something different than a pep talk. It needs to feel safe in the body, not just in the mind.

Why your inner child still lives in that dark room

Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, talks about parts of us that get frozen in time. When trauma happens, parts of our psyche get stuck at the age we were when the wound occurred. So there's a part of you that's still six years old, lying in that dark bedroom, waiting for the footsteps to come.

That part doesn't age. It doesn't learn new information. It doesn't care that you have a mortgage and a job and a partner who loves you. It only knows one thing: the dark means danger, and danger means I might not survive this night.

When you feel that panic rise in a dark room, that's not you being irrational. That's you being faithful to a part of yourself that kept you alive. That part deserves gratitude, not shame. It did its job. It kept you breathing. It got you here.

But here's the hard truth. That part is also keeping you stuck. Because as long as you let it run the show when the lights go out, you never get to learn that darkness can be safe. You never get to rewire that map.

Janina Fisher, who wrote extensively about trauma-informed treatment, calls this the "trauma-related structural dissociation" of the self. We have parts that are stuck in the past, and parts that are trying to function in the present. The goal isn't to get rid of the scared part. The goal is to let the adult part take the lead, while the scared part gets to rest.

I know that sounds abstract. Let me make it concrete.

Last week, I had a power outage. The whole block went dark at 11 PM. My heart started pounding. My hands went cold. I could feel that six-year-old boy climbing into my chest, wanting to hide under the bed. But instead of fighting him or shaming him, I did something different. I said, out loud, "I see you. I know you're scared. But I'm here now. I'm the adult. And I've got this."

It felt ridiculous. It also worked. Not perfectly. Not completely. But the panic dropped from a 9 to a 5. And that's progress.

Practical steps for retraining your nervous system in the dark

So what do you actually do about this? You can't avoid darkness forever. And you shouldn't have to. Here are some things that have helped me, and that might help you too.

First, start small. Don't try to sleep in total darkness on night one if that terrifies you. Use a nightlight. Use a lamp on a dimmer. Let your nervous system acclimate slowly. This isn't about forcing yourself to be brave. It's about building safety, one tiny step at a time.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs. They reduce ambient noise without completely blocking sound, which can help if you're hypervigilant to every creak and footstep. I use them on nights when my nervous system is on high alert, and they make a real difference.

Second, use your breath. I know, I know. Everyone tells you to breathe. But here's the specific technique that works for this situation. Inhale for four counts. Hold for four counts. Exhale for six counts. The longer exhale activates the vagus nerve, which is the brake pedal for your nervous system. Do this for two minutes. It won't fix everything. But it will shift the dial.

Third, bring something familiar into the dark. A specific blanket. A stuffed animal you've had since childhood. A playlist of songs that feel safe. Your brain needs anchors. Things that say: this darkness is different from that darkness. This one is okay.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook. It has exercises specifically designed to help you track triggers and build new responses. I've worked through parts of it myself, and it gave me language for things I couldn't name before.

Fourth, talk to your scared part. Literally. Out loud if you can. Say something like, "I know you're scared right now. I know the dark feels dangerous. But we're not in that house anymore. We're in a different place. And I'm going to keep us safe." It sounds strange. But parts work is real, and it works.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving. Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and inner critics gave me a framework for understanding why the dark triggers such intense reactions. He explains that emotional flashbacks don't have visual components. They're just waves of feeling. And learning to ride those waves instead of drowning in them is a skill you can build.

The ending that doesn't wrap up neatly

I'm not going to tell you that if you just try hard enough, you'll never be scared of the dark again. That's not how this works. Complex trauma doesn't get cured. It gets integrated. It gets understood. It gets held with compassion instead of shame.

Some nights, the dark will still feel big. Some nights, that six-year-old will still climb into your chest and take the wheel. And on those nights, you'll need to be gentle with yourself. You'll need to remember that this reaction is not a failure. It's a sign of how hard you fought to survive.

But other nights? Other nights, you'll notice the darkness feels neutral. Or even peaceful. And you'll realize that your nervous system is learning. Slowly. Imperfectly. But learning.

Sit with that. Let it land. You are not broken. You are not too much. You are a person who survived something hard, and your body is still trying to protect you. The work isn't to shut that protection down. The work is to let your body know, over and over again, that you're safe now. That the dark is just the dark. And that you can handle it.

Even on the nights when it doesn't feel that way.
